title: Never give up, never say never

CJ's life philosophy: 'When a door closes, a much larger and better window opens'
Autobiographical arc â€” UP scholarship lost, FEU door opened, Yale denied due to activism, SC and CJ-ship gained

Yale full scholarship (including board and lodging) â€” US Embassy refused visa due to student activism

Resolution citing his 'core judicial philosophy of safeguarding the liberty and nurturing the prosperity of our people'
Inspired five years after retirement by Ryan Cayabyab's 'Ageless Passion' musical to convert legal lore into practical reality
Initial P2 million seed to FLP treasury; P50M from companies/foundations for Makati 700 sqm head office; another P50M (and counting) for educational projects
Center for Liberty and Prosperity in Metro Manila (and a larger one planned at New Manila International Airport in Bulacan)

signature_phrases.phrase: Life is a strange interplay of light and shadows, of victories and defeats
Amid these zigzags of life, we must keep on trying, keep on fighting
We must never give up; never say 'never'
When a door closes, a much larger and better window opens
I buried myself in work as a virtual hermit, shunning social interaction
The Renaissance Jurist of the 21st Century
the Court's most prolific writer, bar none
the world of hard knocks and grinding poverty
To put our money where our mouth is
help the poor help themselves through viable and sustainable private enterprises
Whatever you do for the least of my brethren, you do unto me

National artist Ryan Cayabyab ('Ageless Passion' composer)
institutions: National Union of Students of the Philippines (NUSP)

1957 â€” CJ's youngest FEUCSO presidency era

stances.claim: Life is a strange interplay of light and shadows, of victories and defeats â€” and the right response is to keep trying, keep fighting, never give up, never say 'never,' because 'when a door closes, a much larger and better window opens.'
stances.rhetorical_move: philosophy-of-life maxim with door/window metaphor as autobiographical lens

stances.claim: CJ's career arc illustrates the door-window principle: UP scholarship granted but unenterable due to 15-centavo Diliman ride; FEU window opened (Salonga and Roces as mentors, FEUCSO and NUSP leadership, cum laude, sixth-place bar); Yale full scholarship with US Embassy visa refusal due to activism; eventual SC appointment, 1,200+ signed decisions, CJ-ship, and the unanimous resolution naming him 'The Renaissance Jurist of the 21st Century' and 'the Court's most prolific writer, bar none.'

Five years after retirement, inspired by Ryan Cayabyab's musical 'Ageless Passion,' CJ converted his judicial philosophy from 'imbedded legal lore' into practical reality â€” moving the philosophy 'outside the intellectual realm of lawyers into the world of hard knocks and grinding poverty' through FLP's seeded P2M, then P50M for the Makati head office, then P50M+ for educational projects.

FLP's two ultimate projects are (1) a Center for Liberty and Prosperity hosting an AI-powered immersive Museum (in partnership with the SC) where visitors enter a virtual SC Session Hall and interact with AI robots mimicking justices â€” 'dedicated to the talented'; and (2) a multibillion-peso Prosperity Fund for MSMEs, nonprofit but financially viable and sustainable, to 'unleash the entrepreneurial ingenuity of our people as the key to eliminating extreme poverty' â€” 'dedicated to the poor,' grounded in Matthew 25:40.
stances.rhetorical_move: two-project ultimate-mission statement with talented-vs-poor pairing

notable_anecdotes.summary: UP scholarship granted but unenterable â€” 'I could not enter UP's door to higher education because my impoverished family could not afford the then measly 15 centavo ride to Diliman, QC' â€” perhaps the single most-vivid datum of CJ's poverty origin.

Yale full scholarship including board and lodging, blocked by US Embassy visa refusal 'due to my student activism' â€” a rare ICE-cold reading of how American institutions screened anti-establishment Filipinos.

'I buried myself in work as a virtual hermit, shunning social interaction' during SC years â€” the workaholic confession behind 1,200+ signed decisions, one book per year, and chairing most SC committees.
Unanimous Court resolution naming him 'The Renaissance Jurist of the 21st Century' and 'the Court's most prolific writer, bar none' â€” given 'without my participation and vote.'

The Cayabyab-musical-as-conversion-moment: 'I was inspired by the musical "Ageless Passion" composed by national artist Ryan Cayabyab to transform my philosophy from being merely an imbedded "legal lore" into a practical reality.'

Donating a P100M+ residential lot in Paseo de Magallanes, Makati 'as a major asset of my family' â€” 'to put our money where our mouth is' â€” to be liquified at the appropriate time for the museum and Prosperity Fund.
Closing scripture: Matthew 25:40 â€” 'Whatever you do for the least of my brethren, you do unto me' â€” as the philanthropy's spiritual anchor.

decision_framework_signals: uses door-window metaphor to recast setbacks as redirected opportunities
treats autobiographical-poverty origin as legitimating his philanthropy mission
structures FLP mission as talented-vs-poor pairing (Museum + Prosperity Fund)
uses 'embedded legal lore vs practical reality' contrast to justify post-judicial philanthropy
anchors philanthropy in scripture (Matthew 25:40) for spiritual legitimation
uses personal-asset donation (Paseo de Magallanes lot) as 'put your money where your mouth is' integrity-proof

one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban delivers a shortened version of his keynote speech at the FLP scholarship ceremony of late July 2023 â€” where Foundation for Liberty and Prosperity awarded 21 legal scholarships (P200,000 each, Tan Yan Kee partnership), five Esmel fellowships (P450,000 each, Metro Pacific partnership), and ten dissertation winners (P1.325M total, Ayala Corp partnership), with CJ Alexander G. Gesmundo as keynote speaker. He frames life as 'a strange interplay of light and shadows, of victories and defeats' and offers his autobiography as evidence of the door-window principle: he won a UP scholarship but couldn't afford the 15-centavo Diliman ride; he therefore enrolled at nearby FEU where he was mentored by Jovito R. Salonga and Alejandro R. Roces, becoming the youngest FEUCSO president, founding NUSP, graduating cum laude, placing sixth in the bar â€” only to be granted a Yale full scholarship that the US Embassy denied a visa for, due to his student activism. He buried himself as 'a virtual hermit' on the SC, producing 1,200+ fully signed decisions, one book per year, chairing most committees, and earning a unanimous Court resolution naming him 'The Renaissance Jurist of the 21st Century' and 'the Court's most prolific writer, bar none.' Five years after retirement, inspired by national artist Ryan Cayabyab's musical 'Ageless Passion,' he resolved to convert his philosophy from 'imbedded legal lore' into 'practical reality... outside the intellectual realm of lawyers into the world of hard knocks and grinding poverty.' He seeded FLP with P2M; companies and foundations he advised gave P50M for the Makati head office and another P50M+ for educational projects. He announces FLP's two 'ultimate projects': a Center for Liberty and Prosperity hosting an interactive, immersive, AI-powered Museum (visitors enter a virtual SC Session Hall and converse with AI robots mimicking justices) dedicated to the talented; and a multibillion-peso Prosperity Fund for MSMEs, nonprofit but financially viable, dedicated to the poor. He donated his family's P100M+ Paseo de Magallanes lot to seed the two projects, anchoring all this in Matthew 25:40: 'Whatever you do for the least of my brethren, you do unto me.'
